upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very stable lower atmosphere with an anomalously weak lapse rate of -3.16°C/km from surface to 850mb — far below dry adiabatic (9.8°C/km) and even moist adiabatic (6.5°C/km), indicating a strong surface-based inversion capping thermals well below 850mb (roughly below 1500ft MSL in the early morning). At 850mb (~5000ft), temps are +15.6°C with a 28°C dewpoint spread, confirming a dry, warm layer aloft that will resist thermal penetration. Winds at 850mb are 11 kts from 280°, veering to 240° at 700mb at 19 kts, with 36 kts from 225° at 500mb — no significant wind shear hazard at flying altitudes, but the thermal ceiling will be modest even after heating, likely topping out near 1200-1500ft AGL by early afternoon before cloud encroachment ends the day.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. METARs unavailable due to data fetch error — exercise additional caution and cross-check local conditions before flight. Buoy data shows strong NW flow offshore: SF Bar buoy reporting 21.4 kts at 320°, Half Moon Bay buoy 17.5 kts at 310° with 1.2m swell at 9s period. Cold SST of 12.2–13.0°C indicates a robust marine layer that may delay coastal clearing. No precipitation forecast at any site today.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas / Diablo Range foothills — NW-facing launch, thermal and valley breeze site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750ft launch uses a combination of thermal lift from the sun-heated east bay foothills and an afternoon sea breeze / valley wind from the NW. Ideal conditions are NW winds 8–15 kts with active thermals; the NW flow provides both ridge and thermal support. Overly light winds mean sink-dominated air; strengthening westerly flow after 3 PM can push the site over limits.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is a narrow usable window squeezed between a calm, thermally-capped morning and an overcast/overstrength afternoon. The strong low-level inversion evident in the sounding suppresses thermal development until mid-morning, and boundary layer height at the 1750ft launch only reaches ~390m (about 1280ft AGL above the valley floor) by late morning — shallow but workable. Winds are light and NW through mid-morning (3–5 kts at 10m), gradually building to 7–8 kts NW by 1 PM, which is the sweet spot. However, cloud cover jumps to 100% by 2 PM and winds climb to 8–10 kts at 10m (with 80m winds reaching 8–12 kts), signaling sea breeze reinforcement and overcast conditions that will flatten thermals and increase turbulence risk. Zero CAPE throughout confirms no convective hazard but also no robust thermals — any lift will be gentle and largely mechanical/convergence-driven. The window is real but brief, and the overcast arrival by 2 PM is a hard stop.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NW 2–5 kts at 10m, boundary layer only 40–340m — thermals not yet established, air is stable and inversion-capped. Soarable for experienced pilots on good cycles only; not recommended for P2.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: winds build to 4–7 kts NW, BL reaches ~390m, skies mostly clear through noon with only 14–21% cloud. Light-moderate thermals possible 11 AM–1 PM; by 1–2 PM clouds build rapidly to 100% cover, signaling the end of the thermal window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% cloud cover arrives by 2 PM; winds at 10m climb to 8–10 kts (NW/WNW), with 80m winds 8–12 kts. Thermals killed by overcast; mechanical/sea-breeze turbulence increases. Not recommended — land before 2 PM.

verdict.hazards: Strong surface inversion caps thermals early; 100% overcast by 2 PM kills lift; winds increase to 10 kts+ after 2 PM with gusty sea breeze; brief window only

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas / Diablo Range — lower training hill launch, NW-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch at Ed Levin is a training hill used for sled rides and beginner flights in light thermic conditions. It depends on the same NW valley breeze and gentle thermals as the main launch but requires lighter, steadier winds. Ideal: NW 5–10 kts, calm to light thermals, no gusts.
sites.todaySetup: Conditions at the 600ft launch mirror the main site but with somewhat more exposure to surface wind variability. The morning is flyable with light NW winds (5–7 kts) and a clearing sky, but thermals are shallow given the strong inversion (BL only 235–335m through mid-morning). The best thermal window is again 11 AM–1 PM with NW winds 5–8 kts — acceptable for confident P2 pilots doing short flights with good LZ access. By 2 PM, 97–100% cloud cover arrives and winds build to 9–11 kts at 10m with 80m winds hitting 10–13 kts, bringing gusty sea-breeze-influenced air that is inappropriate for a training hill. The window is short but viable for supervised P2 flights in the late morning.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW 5–7 kts, BL 90–335m, sky mostly clear by 9 AM. Sled-ride conditions with light cycles; thermals weak but present after 10 AM. OK for supervised P2 with short glide goals.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW 5–8 kts, BL ~360–405m — best window for the 600ft launch. Light thermals, manageable winds, clear skies through noon. Clouds build to near-100% by 2 PM, ending the window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 9–11 kts NW at 10m, 80m winds 10–14 kts, 100% overcast. Too strong and gusty for the training hill; no thermal support. Land by 2 PM.

verdict.hazards: Strong morning inversion limits thermals; 100% overcast and winds building to 11 kts after 2 PM; gusty sea breeze; training hill limits apply

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas / Diablo Range — beginner training hill, NW-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft launch is the bunny hill for student and novice training — pure sled rides in very light winds, typically used for H2/P1-P2 groundwork and short flights into the main LZ. Requires calm to very light winds, no thermic activity of consequence.
sites.todaySetup: This launch has the most forgiving morning window of the Ed Levin complex. Winds are light NW (5–7 kts) from the start, and while thermals are shallow (BL only 70–245m before 10 AM), the 300ft launch doesn't need them — it's a sled-ride hill. The concern is wind direction: winds at 10m are initially NW then trend slightly W-NW through the morning (284–305°), which is slightly cross but generally acceptable for this launch. The same overcast arrival by 2 PM and wind increase to 9–11 kts applies here too, making afternoon operations inadvisable. Morning and late-morning flights are the call.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NW/WNW 5–6 kts, minimal thermals, clear skies by 9 AM. Good sled-ride conditions for supervised students; wind direction slightly variable (284–326°) but manageable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 6–9 kts NW, BL 340–455m — light thermals beginning to develop. Short thermal flights possible for P2; winds acceptable but trending upward toward the practical limit for this launch by 1 PM.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds climb to 9–11 kts at 10m, 100% overcast. Too strong for the 300ft training hill; conditions deteriorate. Clear the hill by 2 PM.

verdict.hazards: Wind increases and full overcast by 2 PM; wind direction variable early; training hill only — no XC or extended soaring; afternoon conditions exceed beginner limits

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site on the Pacific coast bluffs. It needs consistent W to NW onshore flow of 12–20+ kts to generate reliable lift along the ridge face. Too light and pilots sink out; too strong (20+ kts sustained with gusts) and conditions become dangerous with rotor and compression turbulence. Marine layer timing and burn-off are critical — site needs to be clear of fog/cloud for safe operations.
sites.todaySetup: The offshore buoy data tells the core story here: SF Bar buoy is reporting 21.4 kts at 320° and Half Moon Bay buoy 17.5 kts at 310° with cold SST of 12.2–13.0°C. This strong NW onshore flow is excellent for Mussel Rock ridge lift, but the cold ocean temps and active swell (1.2m at 9s at HMB) support a persistent marine layer. The GFS/HRRR show 100% cloud cover at 8 AM clearing to 5% by 10 AM, then rebuilding through the afternoon — suggesting a morning marine layer burn-off window. Winds at the site go from 7–8 kts WNW at 8 AM, building steadily to 10–14 kts NW by 3–4 PM, and reaching 14–15 kts NW by 5 PM with 80m winds near 20 kts. The mid-morning to early afternoon window (10 AM–2 PM) offers 7–10 kts with clearing skies — excellent ridge soaring conditions. By 3–5 PM, winds climb to 12–14 kts at 10m and 17–20 kts at 80m, which is strong but within the site's upper range for experienced pilots. No precipitation, no AIRMETs. This is the best site of the day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Marine layer cloud 100% at 8 AM dropping to 5–16% by 10 AM — wait for burn-off confirmation before launching. Winds 7–8 kts W-WNW; once clear, ridge lift will be active with manageable strength.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: winds 8–10 kts WNW, skies partly cloudy to mostly clear (5–56% cloud), smooth ridge lift expected. 80m winds 7–12 kts — solid soaring with good glide height. Cloud rebuilding by 1–2 PM warrants attention.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 10–15 kts NW, 80m winds 12–20 kts — strong ridge soaring for experienced pilots but approach the upper limits by 5 PM. 100% cloud cover returns by 3 PM; visibility fine but thermic contribution drops.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer may delay launch until 10 AM; winds build to 14+ kts NW by late afternoon with 80m winds 17–20 kts — strong for less experienced pilots; cold SST-driven marine layer may rebound earlier than forecast
verdict.skillLevel: P3+ (afternoon); P2 OK 10 AM–noon with supervision

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont / Diablo Range — NW-facing thermal launch, 2517ft MSL
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a thermal-dominant site relying on sun-heated east bay terrain to generate convective lift. NW component winds of 8–15 kts support both thermal and ridge lift. The site is exposed to the full sea breeze acceleration through the Fremont gap, so afternoon wind increases need to be monitored carefully. Best flying is mid-morning to early afternoon before sea breeze peaks.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same atmospheric environment as Ed Levin — strong morning inversion, shallow BL, and a brief mid-morning window before overcast shuts things down. The 120m winds are notably lighter than at lower levels (1.4–3.9 kts at 120m through the morning, building to 10–12 kts by 3–5 PM), suggesting clean laminar flow aloft during the thermal window. Winds at 10m start NW 2–5 kts, building to 7–10 kts NW by 1–3 PM — right in the sweet spot for the site. However, the same 100% cloud cover arrives by 2 PM and winds increase to 10 kts+ at 10m with 12 kts at 80m by 3 PM, compressing the flyable window. The BL reaches ~390m by late morning, which provides a modest but real thermal ceiling. For a P3 pilot comfortable with the site, there's a 2-hour window around midday. Note that Tomorrow.io data is not available for today at Mission Peak, but HRRR and GFS are in full agreement on this site's forecast.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NW 2–5 kts, BL only 40–340m, skies clearing rapidly to near-zero cloud by 9–10 AM. Thermals developing but weak; marginal soaring until at least 10:30 AM. Not recommended for early launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: NW 4–8 kts, BL ~390m, clear skies through noon. Moderate thermals possible with the improved sun angle; 80m winds 4–8 kts clean. Launch by 11:30 AM for maximum window before overcast arrives.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast by 2 PM, winds increase to 10 kts NW at 10m and 12–14 kts at 80m. Thermals killed; mechanical turbulence increasing with sea breeze push through Fremont gap. Land by 2 PM.

verdict.hazards: Inversion caps thermals early; 100% overcast and strengthening sea breeze by 2 PM compress window severely; Fremont gap acceleration can produce rapid wind increase; no METAR data available for ground-truth

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Thursday March 26 looks unflyable. HRRR/GFS show winds rotating to NE/ENE 11–17 kts at 10m with 80m winds of 20–24 kts — a strong offshore/northerly push. 100% cloud cover all day. Tomorrow.io agrees with strong NE winds developing by afternoon (17 kts at 10m, gusts to 28.6 kts). No thermal potential, wrong wind direction, overcast — NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same story as 1750ft. HRRR shows NE 10–21 kts at 10m, 80m winds 14–29 kts, full overcast. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 27 kts by afternoon. Wrong direction and too strong for training hill — NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: NO FLY. Strong NE winds (12–15 kts at 10m) with gusts to 26+ kts (Tomorrow.io) and full overcast. Training hill operations impossible.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Thursday is problematic for Mussel Rock. Models show variable/light winds in the morning (3–7 kts) shifting NE/E early — an offshore flow pattern that is wrong for ridge soaring. By afternoon, winds may rotate back toward NW (6–7 kts at 10m) but 100% overcast all day. GFS/HRRR agree on low wind speeds; Tomorrow.io diverges significantly showing 13–16 kts NW with gusts to 25 kts in the morning — significant model disagreement. Weight HRRR: marginal-to-no-fly with wrong/light morning winds and fog. Monitor closely.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: NO FLY Thursday. Mirrors Ed Levin — strong NE/ENE 11–17 kts at 10m, 80m winds 20–24 kts, 100% overcast all day. Wrong direction for the site, too strong, no thermal support.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Friday March 27: winds calm considerably — NW 3–7 kts at 10m, but 100% cloud cover persists all day with BL values ranging wildly (75–825m). Thermal potential exists if skies clear (BL reaches 800m by afternoon) but models keep it fully overcast. MARGINAL at best, conditional on actual cloud break — check morning conditions.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Friday March 27 similar to 1750ft — light NW winds, overcast all day. BL shows potential but clouds prevent thermal development. MARGINAL/NO FLY depending on actual clearing.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Friday March 27: very light winds (3–5 kts NW) and 100% overcast most of the day. Sled rides possible if cloud base is high enough, but visibility and cloud coverage make this uncertain. Monitor.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Friday March 27: model shows light and variable winds (4–6 kts, variable direction including SE/E in the morning), 100% overcast. Too light and wrong direction for ridge soaring in the morning; a slight NW sea breeze may develop by afternoon (4–6 kts) but remains weak under full cloud. Unlikely flyable.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Friday March 27: light to calm winds (3–7 kts NW), full overcast, BL up to 800m by afternoon. If skies clear (uncertain), there could be moderate thermals. Currently NO FLY on clouds alone; watch for late clearing.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Saturday March 28: Possibly the best day of the extended forecast for Ed Levin. Winds calm early (near calm at 8 AM), then NW 3–8 kts building through the day. BL reaches 895m by 2 PM — excellent thermal ceiling. Cloud cover is 100% all day in the model, which is the big question mark. If actual clearing occurs (as it sometimes does with these setups when surface heating wins out), this could be a good thermal day. Watch morning satellite for early signs.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Saturday March 28: Same favorable setup as 1750ft — light NW 3–8 kts, BL reaching 895m, calm early morning. If clouds break, strong thermals possible. Currently conditional/MARGINAL on actual cloud coverage.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Saturday March 28: Light winds, deep BL potential. Training hill would benefit from clearing. Same cloud uncertainty as upper launches.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Saturday March 28: Winds start near calm then build to 5–6 kts W/WNW by afternoon. Very light for ridge soaring — likely insufficient for sustained lift. 100% overcast all day per models. NO FLY unless unexpected wind increase occurs.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Saturday March 28: Similar to Ed Levin — light winds (2–5 kts NW), BL potentially reaching 975m by 2 PM (deepest of the 4-day period), full model cloud cover. If clouds clear, this could be an excellent thermal day for P3+ pilots. Conditional MARGINAL — watch morning satellite.

watchlist: Marine layer burn-off at Mussel Rock: cold SST (12.2°C) and active NW swell (1.2m/9s) support persistent coastal cloud — confirm visual clearing before launching, targeting 10 AM at earliest
100% overcast arrival by 2 PM at all inland sites today kills thermals and marks the hard stop for flying — do not extend sessions past 1:30 PM
Sea breeze acceleration: winds at Ed Levin and Mission Peak build to 10+ kts at 10m by 2–3 PM with 80m winds reaching 12–14 kts — the afternoon ramp-up is rapid once sea breeze establishes
Thursday looks completely unflyable at all sites due to strong NE/offshore flow 11–21 kts and full overcast — rest day

Model disagreement for Mussel Rock Thursday: Tomorrow.io shows 13–16 kts NW (flyable) vs HRRR 3–7 kts variable (not flyable) — do not commit to a Thursday Mussel Rock session without morning condition check

Strong surface inversion today (lapse rate only -3.16°C/km sfc to 850mb) means thermals will be weak and short-lived; do not expect more than 1000–1200ft AGL above launch height even during the best window
METARs unavailable — pilots should obtain ground-truth surface observations independently before flight, especially at coastal sites
tldr: Mussel Rock is today's star — the NW flow is dialed in with 8–12 kts on the ridge once the marine layer burns off around 10 AM, giving a solid 4-hour window. Inland sites (Ed Levin all launches, Mission Peak) have a brief thermal window 11 AM–1:30 PM before 100% overcast shuts everything down hard. Thursday is a rest day everywhere — strong NE flow and full overcast make all sites NO FLY.
